instruction: How do I use Chrome DevTools to profile a React application and find rendering performance bottlenecks?
output:
React performance profiling has two layers: (1) Chrome DevTools Performance panel for JavaScript execution and rendering, and (2) React DevTools Profiler for component-level rendering analysis. Chrome Performance panel records a timeline showing: JS execution (yellow), rendering/layout (purple), painting (green), and idle time (gray). The key metrics are: Long Tasks (>50ms blocks), Layout Thrashing (forced reflows), and excessive re-renders. React DevTools Profiler shows: which components rendered, why they rendered (props changed, state changed, parent rendered), and how long each render took. The flame chart view shows the component tree with render times. Common React bottlenecks: (1) unnecessary re-renders (parent renders, child re-renders even though props didn't change — fix with React.memo), (2) expensive renders (large lists without virtualization), (3) state updates in loops (batch them), (4) layout thrashing from reading DOM after writing. The Profiler API (`` component) can be used programmatically to log render timings in production (with sampling). ```typescript import React, { useState, useMemo, useCallback, Profiler } from 'react'; // === React DevTools Profiler: component-level timing === // onRender callback — log slow renders function onRenderCallback( id: string, // the "id" prop of the Profiler tree phase: 'mount' | 'update', actualDuration: number, // time spent rendering this update baseDuration: number, // estimated time for full re-render startTime: number, commitTime: number, ) { if (actualDuration > 16) { // slower than 60fps frame budget console.warn( `Slow render: ${id} (${phase}) took ${actualDuration.toFixed(1)}ms` ); } } // Wrap components in to measure function App() { return ( ); } // === Common bottleneck: unnecessary re-renders === // BAD: re-renders on every parent render function BadItem({ item, onClick }: { item: any; onClick: (id: string) => void }) { console.log(`Rendering ${item.id}`); // fires on EVERY parent render return
onClick(item.id)}>{item.name}
; } // GOOD: memoized, only re-renders when props change const GoodItem = React.memo(function GoodItem( { item, onClick }: { item: any; onClick: (id: string) => void } ) { console.log(`Rendering ${item.id}`); // only when item or onClick changes return
onClick(item.id)}>{item.name}
; }); // === Expensive list with proper optimization === function ExpensiveList({ count }: { count: number }) { const [filter, setFilter] = useState(''); const [selected, setSelected] = useState(null); // useMemo: don't recompute filtered list on every render const items = useMemo(() => { const all = Array.from({ length: count }, (_, i) => ({ id: `item-${i}`, name: `Item ${i}`, })); return filter ? all.filter(item => item.name.toLowerCase().includes(filter)) : all; }, [count, filter]); // useCallback: stable reference so memoized children don't re-render const handleClick = useCallback((id: string) => { setSelected(id); }, []); return (
setFilter(e.target.value)} placeholder="Filter..." />
Selected: {selected}
{items.map(item => ( ))}
); } // === Chrome DevTools Performance recording steps === // 1. Open DevTools > Performance tab // 2. Click Record (or Cmd+Shift+E) // 3. Interact with the app (scroll, click, type) // 4. Stop recording // 5. Look for: // - Long Tasks (red bars > 50ms) — blocks main thread // - Layout shifts (purple bars) — forced reflows // - Excessive scripting (yellow) — JS computation // 6. Click on any bar to see the call stack export default App; ```

instruction: How do I use the webpack-bundle-analyzer to find what's making my JavaScript bundle so large?
output:
<think>
Bundle size directly impacts load time. webpack-bundle-analyzer generates an interactive treemap visualization showing every module in your bundle, sized proportionally. You can immediately see which dependencies are huge.

Common findings: (1) moment.js with all locales (>500KB — switch to date-fns or dayjs), (2) lodash fully imported instead of lodash-es with tree-shaking, (3) duplicate copies of the same library at different versions, (4) source maps or dev-only code in production, (5) polyfills for browsers you don't support.

The workflow: add the plugin to webpack config, build, and it opens a browser with the treemap. Each rectangle is a module; size represents its contribution to the bundle. Click to drill into node_modules to see which packages are largest.

For production analysis, use `--mode production` to see the actual minified+gzipped sizes. The analyzer shows three sizes: stat (raw), parsed (after transforms), and gzip (what users download).

Alternatives: `source-map-explorer` for simpler analysis, `bundlephobia.com` for checking package sizes before installing.
</think>

```typescript
// === webpack.config.js setup ===
const BundleAnalyzerPlugin = require('webpack-bundle-analyzer').BundleAnalyzerPlugin;

module.exports = {
  plugins: [
    // Opens interactive treemap in browser after build
    new BundleAnalyzerPlugin({
      analyzerMode: 'static',  // generates HTML file
      reportFilename: 'bundle-report.html',
      openAnalyzer: true,
      defaultSizes: 'gzip',   // show gzipped sizes (what users download)
    }),
  ],
};

// === For Next.js ===
// next.config.js
const withBundleAnalyzer = require('@next/bundle-analyzer')({
  enabled: process.env.ANALYZE === 'true',
});
module.exports = withBundleAnalyzer({ /* next config */ });
// Run: ANALYZE=true npm run build

// === Common fixes after analysis ===

// FIX 1: Replace moment.js (500KB+) with date-fns (tree-shakeable)
// BAD:
import moment from 'moment'; // imports ALL locales
// GOOD:
import { format, parseISO } from 'date-fns'; // only imports what you use

// FIX 2: Import only what you need from lodash
// BAD:
import _ from 'lodash'; // entire library (>70KB gzipped)
const result = _.debounce(fn, 300);
// GOOD:
import debounce from 'lodash/debounce'; // just this one function (~1KB)

// FIX 3: Dynamic imports for heavy, rarely-used features
// BAD: loaded on every page
import { PDFViewer } from 'heavy-pdf-library';
// GOOD: loaded only when user clicks "View PDF"
const PDFViewer = React.lazy(() => import('heavy-pdf-library'));

// FIX 4: Check for duplicate packages
// npx duplicate-package-checker-webpack-plugin
// Or: npm ls <package-name> to see duplicate installations

// FIX 5: Externalize large deps served from CDN
module.exports = {
  externals: {
    react: 'React',
    'react-dom': 'ReactDOM',
  },
};

// === Size budget enforcement ===
module.exports = {
  performance: {
    maxAssetSize: 250_000,       // 250KB per asset
    maxEntrypointSize: 500_000,  // 500KB for entry point
    hints: 'error',              // fail build if exceeded
  },
};
```

instruction: How do I detect and fix memory leaks in a Node.js application using Chrome DevTools memory timeline?
output:
<think>
Node.js memory leaks are insidious because they're slow — the process grows over hours/days until it OOMs. Common causes: event listeners not removed, closures holding references, growing Maps/Sets used as caches without eviction, global variables accumulating data.

The debugging workflow: (1) Start Node with `--inspect` flag, (2) Open `chrome://inspect` in Chrome, (3) Take heap snapshots at intervals, (4) Compare snapshots to find growing object counts.

The heap snapshot comparison is the killer feature. Take snapshot A (baseline), run load for a while, force GC, take snapshot B. In the "Comparison" view, look for object types with growing counts — those are potential leaks. Sort by "# Delta" to find what's accumulating.

The "Allocation Timeline" recording is even better for tracking when and where allocations happen. It shows allocation stacks in real-time, so you can correlate leaks with specific code paths.

For production, use `process.memoryUsage()` to monitor RSS and heapUsed over time. If heapUsed grows monotonically, you have a leak. Use `--max-old-space-size` to set a limit and catch leaks earlier.
</think>

```typescript
// === Start with inspect flag ===
// node --inspect --max-old-space-size=512 server.js
// Then open chrome://inspect in Chrome

// === Common leak patterns and fixes ===

import { EventEmitter } from 'events';

// LEAK 1: Event listeners accumulating
class LeakyService {
  private emitter = new EventEmitter();

  handleRequest(req: any) {
    // BAD: adds a NEW listener on every request — never removed!
    this.emitter.on('data', (data) => {
      this.processData(data, req);
    });
  }

  processData(data: any, req: any) { /* ... */ }
}

class FixedService {
  private emitter = new EventEmitter();

  handleRequest(req: any) {
    // GOOD: use once() or remove listener when done
    const handler = (data: any) => this.processData(data, req);
    this.emitter.once('data', handler); // auto-removes after one call
  }

  processData(data: any, req: any) { /* ... */ }
}

// LEAK 2: Unbounded cache
const cache = new Map<string, any>(); // grows forever!

function leakyLookup(key: string): any {
  if (!cache.has(key)) {
    cache.set(key, expensiveCompute(key)); // never evicted
  }
  return cache.get(key);
}

// FIX: Use LRU cache with max size
class LRUCache<K, V> {
  private cache = new Map<K, V>();
  constructor(private maxSize: number) {}

  get(key: K): V | undefined {
    const value = this.cache.get(key);
    if (value !== undefined) {
      // Move to end (most recently used)
      this.cache.delete(key);
      this.cache.set(key, value);
    }
    return value;
  }

  set(key: K, value: V): void {
    this.cache.delete(key);
    this.cache.set(key, value);
    if (this.cache.size > this.maxSize) {
      // Delete oldest (first entry)
      const oldest = this.cache.keys().next().value!;
      this.cache.delete(oldest);
    }
  }
}

// LEAK 3: Closures holding large objects
function leakyMiddleware() {
  const hugeBuffer = Buffer.alloc(10_000_000); // 10MB

  return (req: any, res: any, next: any) => {
    // Closure captures hugeBuffer even if not used in most paths
    if (req.special) {
      res.send(hugeBuffer);
    } else {
      next();
    }
  };
}

// === Monitoring in production ===
function monitorMemory() {
  setInterval(() => {
    const usage = process.memoryUsage();
    console.log(JSON.stringify({
      rss_mb: (usage.rss / 1024 / 1024).toFixed(1),
      heap_used_mb: (usage.heapUsed / 1024 / 1024).toFixed(1),
      heap_total_mb: (usage.heapTotal / 1024 / 1024).toFixed(1),
      external_mb: (usage.external / 1024 / 1024).toFixed(1),
    }));
  }, 30_000); // every 30s
}

function expensiveCompute(key: string) { return { data: key }; }
```

instruction: How do I use Lighthouse CI to automate web performance auditing and catch regressions before they hit production?
output:
<think>
Lighthouse CI runs Lighthouse audits in CI/CD pipelines and fails the build if performance budgets are exceeded. It's the automated version of manually running Lighthouse in Chrome DevTools.

The setup: (1) install `@lhci/cli`, (2) create `lighthouserc.js` config with performance budgets, (3) add to CI pipeline. LHCI can collect results, assert against budgets, and upload to a server for historical tracking.

Performance budgets are the key — you set thresholds for: Performance score (>= 90), FCP (First Contentful Paint < 1.5s), LCP (Largest Contentful Paint < 2.5s), TBT (Total Blocking Time < 200ms), CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift < 0.1). If any metric exceeds the budget, the CI build fails.

For meaningful results: (1) run against a production-like build (not dev server), (2) run multiple times and take median (Lighthouse results vary by ±5%), (3) test key user flows (homepage, product page, checkout), not just the landing page.

LHCI can also upload results to a Lighthouse server for trend tracking, or to temporary public URLs for PR review.
</think>

```typescript
// === lighthouserc.js ===
module.exports = {
  ci: {
    collect: {
      // URLs to audit
      url: [
        'http://localhost:3000/',
        'http://localhost:3000/products',
        'http://localhost:3000/checkout',
      ],
      numberOfRuns: 5,  // median of 5 runs reduces variance
      startServerCommand: 'npm run start:prod', // start the app
      startServerReadyPattern: 'Listening on port',
    },
    assert: {
      assertions: {
        // Performance budgets — fail CI if exceeded
        'categories:performance': ['error', { minScore: 0.9 }],
        'categories:accessibility': ['warn', { minScore: 0.9 }],
        'categories:best-practices': ['warn', { minScore: 0.9 }],

        // Core Web Vitals
        'first-contentful-paint': ['error', { maxNumericValue: 1500 }], // 1.5s
        'largest-contentful-paint': ['error', { maxNumericValue: 2500 }], // 2.5s
        'total-blocking-time': ['error', { maxNumericValue: 200 }],     // 200ms
        'cumulative-layout-shift': ['error', { maxNumericValue: 0.1 }],

        // Resource budgets
        'resource-summary:script:size': ['error', { maxNumericValue: 300000 }], // 300KB JS
        'resource-summary:total:size': ['error', { maxNumericValue: 1000000 }], // 1MB total
      },
    },
    upload: {
      target: 'temporary-public-storage', // free, generates shareable URL
      // Or use your own LHCI server:
      // target: 'lhci',
      // serverBaseUrl: 'https://lhci.example.com',
    },
  },
};

// === GitHub Actions workflow ===
/*
# .github/workflows/lighthouse.yml
name: Lighthouse CI
on: [pull_request]
jobs:
  lighthouse:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run build
      - name: Lighthouse CI
        run: |
          npm install -g @lhci/cli
          lhci autorun
        env:
          LHCI_GITHUB_APP_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.LHCI_GITHUB_APP_TOKEN }}
*/

// === Custom performance monitoring in code ===
if (typeof window !== 'undefined' && 'PerformanceObserver' in window) {
  // Track LCP in production
  const lcpObserver = new PerformanceObserver((list) => {
    const entries = list.getEntries();
    const lastEntry = entries[entries.length - 1];
    console.log('LCP:', lastEntry.startTime, 'ms');
    // Send to analytics
  });
  lcpObserver.observe({ type: 'largest-contentful-paint', buffered: true });

  // Track CLS
  let clsValue = 0;
  const clsObserver = new PerformanceObserver((list) => {
    for (const entry of list.getEntries() as any[]) {
      if (!entry.hadRecentInput) {
        clsValue += entry.value;
      }
    }
    console.log('CLS:', clsValue);
  });
  clsObserver.observe({ type: 'layout-shift', buffered: true });
}
```
